data.title: Mitek Systems: How Its AI Fortress Protects 50% Of U.S Banks
data.description:
Mitek Systems (MITK) launched its Check Fraud Defender which is AI cloud platform inspecting Cheque forgery in real-time, yet the company's real value is in its constirium which covers over 50% of U.S checking banks, as in the traditional system if a bank discovered a fraud attempt of a forged check it will only protect itself but with using MITK's consortium, this info will be sent to its cloud platform within a fraction of second so all other banks in the constirium will know about it. This advantage creates a strong moat for Mitek and makes it harder for competitors, as the more banks join the consortium, the more powerful the system becomes and also the less able fraudsters are to penetrate it. This makes Mitek an indispensable strategic partner for banks and pay it subscriptions for life.

Overview

During Q1 2026, the company recorded SaaS revenues of 21% growth YoY, which represents 43% of total revenues of the last 12 months and suggests that the company is moving away from the old licensing model in favor of a SaaS model, justifying giving it a higher valuation than it is.

The reports indicate that global digital identity is expected to reach $32 billion by 2028. While the financial fraud sector is undergoing a difficult situation, GenAI made it easier for fraudsters to devise new ways to bypass security systems at even lower cost, leading to an attack flood on banks. According to Deloitte, AI fraud in the U.S. could reach $40 billion by 2027, demonstrating the urgent need for financial institutions to have the latest protection systems.

Despite the decline in the use of paper checks worldwide, cheque fraud is on the rise as banks have not paid it enough attention compared to protecting credit cards and bank transactions. Gangs use what is called Check Washing through a chemical process, as they erase the beneficiary name or the amount from the real check and use a fake one. The Defender solution performs an advanced visual inspection of over 20 attributes of the real check via AI.

Identity Solutions

The company also offers its digital identity products such as MiVIP, IDLive Face, and IDLive Voice, which are among the best in their class, as Mitek can determine whether the user's face or voice is for a real person or not. What distinguishes MITK's strategy is its focus on reducing customer contact, which attracts more customers to the partners; at the same time it raises the protection walls against fraudsters. The management stated that it reached a 90% automation rate, which means every 9 out of 10 users can open bank accounts without human review, reducing operation costs significantly and making switching to Mitek's products an obvious decision for any bank manager.

Financials & Outlook

In Q1, the company announced total revenues of $44.2 million with 19% growth YoY exceeding expectations of $42.4 million, driven by Fraud & Identity solutions which represents 57% of total business with $25.5 million and 30% growth YoY, meaning that MITK's core growth engine significantly accelerates compared to the traditional check verification segment, which also showed resilience and stability with revenues of $18.8 million. The Adjusted EBITDA also grew by %69, this significant growth reflects operating leverage as when revenue increases, the costs will remain constant at the same rate, thus increasing profits, as the profit margin reached 30% vs 21.1% last year.

The company also turned a loss of $4.6 million last year, achieving $2.8 million in the same period, demonstrating Mitek's ability to achieve real profits.

Moreover, the management raised its guidance for 2026, as it expects revenues between $187 and $197 million and also raised its guidance for Adjusted EBITDA to nearly 30%, indicating that the company has a clear vision of its backlog orders, mitigating the risk of sudden profit decline. Additionally, it places it among top tier software companies that apply the 40 Rule, enhancing its valuation.

Value Per Share

MITK trades at a forward EV/EBITDA of 11x. However, the sector median is approximately 14x. Considering the full year guidance expecting average revenues of 192 million and adjusted EBITDA nearly 30%. I will assume EBITDA of $57 million. Companies that achieve rule of 40 usually trade at higher multiples; however, by applying a multiple of 13x to put a margin of safety, The EV will approach $741 million, adding cash & equivalents of $191.8 million and subtracting total debt of $159.2 million, the equity value comes to $774 million or $17 per share, representing potential upside of 15%.

Risks To Consider

Although the promising future, the AI-identity field is crowded and rapidly evolving, as modern technologies today could be outdated tomorrow. Therefore, it may be overtaken by fraudsters using newer AI techs. However, Mitek is aware of this risk and plans to increase its R&D spending sequentially during 2026 in order to protect its economic moat.

In addition, biometric privacy laws are strict and require explicit written consent, this law imposes harsh fines of up to $5000 per violation, and the company already found itself embroiled in some of these lawsuits, such as a case with HyerCar, forwhich Mitek was providing it a service as a third-party behind the scenes.

Final Thoughts

Given Mitek's excellent quarter and guidance, we find that the upside potential far outweighs the downside risks, plus the company's buyback program of $50 million signals the upside trend. However, we need to ensure the continuation of SaaS revenue growth, which reached 43% during Q1. Therefore, its approach to the 50% mark can force the market, giving it higher multiples as a SaaS company.

data.title: European CRE Total Returns Accelerate Into 2026
data.description:
By Rich Hill, Global Head of Real Estate Research & Strategy

European real estate fund returns accelerated into year-end 2025, with selectivity remaining a key driver of performance. European ODCE indices have now posted seven consecutive quarters of positive returns and continue to outperform their U.S. peers, where dispersion between top- and bottom-quartile funds has been more pronounced. In addition, focused residential and retail strategies in Europe are outperforming multisector peers, which are likely to have greater exposure to the still-lagging office sector.

Open-ended core fund returns accelerate

Driven by higher capital returns, the European ODCE index posted net cash returns of +1.12% (local currency) in 4Q25, its seventh consecutive positive quarter. Returns accelerated from +0.83% in 3Q25 as capital returns improved to +0.37%. For 2025 as a whole, the index returned +3.7%, supported primarily by income (+3.1%) and modestly positive capital returns (+0.6%).

In the U.S., the ODCE index posted a +0.7% net return in 4Q25, driven by income (+0.79%) and slightly offset by negative capital returns (-0.1%). Full-year net returns reached +2.9%, with income (+3.2%) outweighing softer capital performance (-0.3%). Historically, the U.S. ODCE index has outperformed Europe in roughly two-thirds of quarters since 3Q11, with annualized returns of +6.4% versus +3.4%. However, this trend has reversed since early 2023, with Europe outperforming in nine of the past twelve quarters.

The recent outperformance of Europe is consistent with our view that the Eurozone is benefitting from an improving economic outlook and lower sovereign debt yields, making leverage more accretive even as cap rates are tighter. Indeed, as of 3Q25, Europe’s unlevered asset-level total returns across all property and strategy types stood at +6.8% over the trailing four quarters, compared to +4.9% for the U.S. as of 4Q25. This is in line with forecasts of 6–7% in Europe versus 5% in the U.S. for 2025.

The other factor at play: fund performance dispersion

Dispersion in returns across funds remains an important narrative for investors. Over the trailing 1-year period, the median net return for European ODCE funds was +4.4%, with the upper quartile at +5.9% and the bottom quartile at +2.7%. By comparison, the top quartile of U.S. ODCE funds posted 1-year annualized gross total returns of +5.8% vs. 4.9% for the 2nd quartile, ~4% for the 3rd quartile, and -0.2% for the bottom quartile. In other words, the top U.S. funds are outperforming in line with Europe, but the bottom funds are underperforming their European peers.

Focused strategies continue to outperform diversified

The broader INREV All-Fund Index delivered a stronger +1.2% total return, supported by income (+1.16%) and capital growth (+0.09%). This is consistent with history, where the All-Fund index has outperformed ODCE in nearly 74% of quarters since 3Q11. A driver of this outperformance continues to be focused residential (+1.8%) and retail (+1.6%) strategies posting stronger results than multi-sector funds (+1.1% total returns). Additionally, on a trailing 4-quarter basis, all focused strategies, except office, meaningfully outperformed their multisector peers.

Bottom line

The momentum behind European core real estate is now firmly rooted in fundamentals: an improving macro backdrop, stronger asset-level performance, and outperformance in focused sectors such as residential and retail. At the same time, widening dispersion across funds, especially within the U.S. ODCE universe, underscores how much manager and strategy selection are shaping investor returns. Taken together, the data point to a market where fundamentals, focus, and manager selection, and not geography alone, are emerging as the primary drivers of outperformance.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Relay Therapeutics: Breakthrough Designation Sets The Stage For A Key Zovegalisib Data Catalyst
data.description:
Thesis

Relay Therapeutics (RLAY) reported earnings in late February. My initial thoughts were that the numbers are pretty much as you would expect. We saw that spending is still very high with the ongoing zovegalisib trials. However, there is an upcoming data readout that I think could actually add some good stock momentum here. The catalyst I think could serve investors well would be zovegalisib, which is tipped to come on March 16th. It's the data readout at ESMO TAT, which should be the first look we get at efficacy and safety at the Phase 3 dose. I think the event could create a nice setup for Relay in 2026, where they have improved financial visibility and potentially a positive clinical update, which should add fresh momentum to the shares.

Financial results

Relay’s 4Q25/FY25 financial results show us a bit of strategic cost management, but also continued investment in their lead development programs, particularly zovegalisib, which is what we want to see. For 4Q25, the company reported revenue of about $7.0 million, a pretty notable increase from the near-zero revenue we saw back in 2024. So we’re starting to see those milestones under its Exclusive License Agreement with Elevar Therapeutics.

As for FY25, revenue managed to hit $15.4 million, up from about $10.0 million in 2024. Now, this is still pretty modest relative to the company’s ongoing R&D spending, but it does show us some improvement. As you know, the big spend is coming from R&D; however, this did decrease slightly to $55.4 million for 4Q25 and $261.4 million for FY25. For context, these figures are down from $68.1 million and $319.1 million, respectively, in 2024. I think we can put these reductions down to a lot of organisational streamlining from Relay. We've also seen the transfer of certain development programs, such as lirafugratinib, to Elevar, which clearly helped lessen the load for them. However, there were increases related to zovegalisib’s Phase 3 ReDiscover-2 trial, as we would expect, and also the ReInspire vascular anomalies trial. G&A expenses also declined somewhat, bringing 4Q25 G&A down to just $12.2 million and FY25 G&A to $56.7 million.

Now, despite these cost controls, Relay still recorded a net loss of about $54.9 million for the quarter, or $0.32 per share. But this is an improvement when you compare it to the $76.0 million loss back in 4Q24. For the year, the net loss was about $276.5 million, or $1.61 per share, again better when compared to the 2024 figures of $337.7 million. So this decline in net loss tells me that there's some improved operational efficiency overall, but the company is still very much investment-focused. I also expect this investment to continue in 2026.

Relay needs to continue prioritising clinical development over near-term profitability in 2026. Cash, cash equivalents, and investments ended the year at a hefty $554.5 million, down slightly from $781.3 million in 2024, but as per management's expectations, this should support a runway into 2029. Just briefly, if we take the FY25 operational spend figure of $318 million and use it as a proxy for cash burn, it gives them about 7 quarters of runway over about 1.7 years. So, based on management's expectations, we can expect spending to undergo more cost control. Overall, I think the results still show us that Relay is in the midst of a high-investment, pivotal clinical phase. They’re trying to balance disciplined expense management with significant ongoing commitments, especially the advancement of zovegalisib in both metastatic breast cancer and PIK3CA-driven vascular anomalies.

Zovegalisib trajectory

Since my last coverage of Relay back in late January, there have been some major developments in the zovegalisib program. The most notable of which is in metastatic breast cancer, with the FDA granting them Breakthrough Therapy Designation for zovegalisib in combination with fulvestrant. This is for adults with PIK3CA-mutant, HR+/HER2- advanced breast cancer who have progressed on or after a CDK4/6 inhibitor. Now, this applicability criterion is a bit lengthy, but it essentially means this treatment is intended for patients with hormone-receptor–positive, HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer, specifically the tumours that carry a PIK3CA mutation. It is also for those in whom the disease has already stopped responding to standard first-line therapy with a CDK4/6 inhibitor. That could be treatments such as palbociclib, ribociclib, or abemaciclib. So, in other words, it targets a molecularly defined group of patients who have relapsed after the most common frontline regimen. It’s a tricky place for patients to be in since there are currently very few options in this area, and outcomes are poor.

I think this designation is a pretty strong regulatory signal. In the sense that early clinical data in this area could indicate a potential for substantial improvement over existing therapies. The designation doesn’t just give them access to all Fast Track features, but it also allows for more engagement with senior FDA leadership. So that should give them some guidance on trial design, which could accelerate the path toward potential approval.

So far, zovegalisib remains the lead clinical asset in Relay’s pipeline, with the potential to target both HR+/HER2- breast cancer and PIK3CA-driven vascular anomalies. The Breakthrough Therapy application relied a lot on the Phase 1/2 ReDiscover trial, which tested two dosing regimens: a 600mg BID fasted option for about 52 patients and a 400mg BID fed option for 57 patients. The latter dosing option here is the recommended Phase 3 dose currently being used in ReDiscover-2. The study was designed to assess safety, tolerability, pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, as you would expect, but also preliminary antitumor activity, which included combination cohorts with CDK inhibitors. The data from the 600mg fasted cohort were presented back at ASCO 2025 and SABCS 2025 and showed a median progression-free survival of about 10.3 months overall. It also showed an ORR of 39%, and a generally a well manageable safety profile consistent with mutant-selective PI3Kα inhibition.

The next key data readout is set to come on March 16th this year, and I’m covering here what I feel we could see in the readout, as I think it could add to the stock momentum already seen on the back of zovegalisib optimism. It’s tipped to be announced at the European Society for Medical Oncology Targeted Anticancer Therapies (ESMO TAT) Congress. This presentation will first feature the Phase 1/2 results for the 400mg BID fed dose but also the Phase 3 dose, focusing on CDK4/6-experienced patients. Specifically, this will be the first look at efficacy/safety data at the actual Phase 3 dose, which is 400 mg BID fed. Until now, the market has mainly been building momentum on data from the 600 mg BID fasted cohort.

You see, this cohort is particularly important since it actually aligns with the intended pivotal patient population for ReDiscover-2. Investors and, of course, FDA will be closely watching several data points here. First, the efficacy signal at the Phase 3 dose; second, the tolerability in combination with fulvestrant; and third, outcomes in key subgroups, including those patients with prior SERD exposure or detectable ESR1 mutations. Now, that last data point is pretty important because prior SERD treatment and ESR1 mutations are both big markers of endocrine resistance. So it means those tumours are much harder to treat and typically respond poorly to standard therapies. So if we do see zovegalisib showing strong activity in these subgroups, it tells me the drug can overcome key resistance mechanisms, adding to the clinical value later on. It would also make it far more competitive versus existing PI3K inhibitors. So positive results at this presentation could end up being a particularly strong catalyst, validating the Phase 3 dosing strategy.

However, we also need to keep in mind that the data package is not yet complete. Yes, the Phase 1/2 results provide promising early evidence, but full confirmation will require the ongoing ReDiscover-2 Phase 3 trial and future triplet cohorts combining zovegalisib with other CDK4/6 inhibitors. So realistically, we are still quite early on in the development. But I think the FDA will likely appreciate the selective inhibition profile here with the combined manageable toxicity. And just to touch briefly on the potential concerns, these could be on the durability of response across all PIK3CA mutations. We also don’t want to see any off-target toxicity. Additionally, how well the Phase 3 data compares with current SOC options, such as alpelisib or other PI3K inhibitors, should determine the strength of this catalyst. So overall, the program is at a pretty pivotal stage, with this March 16th presentation tipped to be a big inflection point.

Valuation

Relay shares currently trade at about $9.85, reflecting a market cap of $1.75 billion. Now, if we take the fact that the company is still in the pre-commercial stage and essentially has no meaningful revenue, traditional valuation metrics like ratios are not really applicable. But this is typical for a clinical-stage biotech, so the best valuation basis here is on enterprise value relative to its cash and future potential. In that sense, Relay has an enterprise value of about $1.23 billion against $554.5 million in cash and cash equivalents. This gives them a net cash position of nearly $522 million. As for price to sales, the metrics are extremely elevated, with TTM and forward price-to-sales ratios of 109x and 330x, respectively, which goes to show the absence of significant current revenue.

Similarly, EV to sales ratios are a bit out of proportion at 80x TTM and 231x forward. The company also carries quite a modest debt figure of $32.5 million, which is pretty much dwarfed by its cash position. So, they have a strong liquidity buffer here to support ongoing clinical programs. In this sense, the valuation here is mainly driven by its pipeline potential, especially for Zovegalisib in metastatic breast cancer and PIK3CA-driven vascular anomalies.

Looking ahead

So I feel zovegalisib is pretty well positioned as the lead asset in Relay’s pipeline. The regulatory momentum from its Breakthrough Therapy Designation and an upcoming look at the highly relevant Phase 1/2 data catalyst at ESMO TAT should help validate the Phase 3 dose and population. We have to keep in mind that the program targets a pretty large and well-defined commercial opportunity here. Management has put estimates of about 140,000 patients per year in the U.S. with HR+/HER2- breast cancer harbouring a PIK3CA mutation. In addition to this, about 170,000 patients have PIK3CA-driven vascular anomalies. So that goes to show you the potential scale if we see efficacy and tolerability continue to differentiate from the earlier PI3K inhibitors. So the bull case here is that the March 16th data readout should act as near-term momentum in a much longer trajectory. It needs to confirm dose selection and reinforce the overall Phase 3 strategy. This should help de-risk the path toward registration.

data.title: KKR Real Estate Finance: The Preferred Shares Are Attractive At Yields Above 9%
data.description:
Thesis

We have not covered the Series A Preferred Shares (KREF.PR.A) from the KKR Real Estate Finance Trust Inc. (KREF) for a while, with our last coverage almost two years ago. At the time we were too eager to call a bottom in the CRE market and in long-term yields, with those factors taking a longer time to normalize.

In today's article we are going to revisit KREF.PR.A, in light of the fund's Q4 2025 results and latest macro outlook.

Results for Q4 2025

Let us start by looking at the fund's results for Q4 2025:

The fund had a net loss, mainly driven by further provisions for credit losses of $43.7 M. While the new provision comes a bit as a surprise considering the history, ultimately we will look into why it is beneficial. The history looks as follows:

We can see that in September 2025 the fund had almost no provisions, with the $43 M number roughly a third of the 2025 fiscal year provisions. The provisions come from two distinct properties:

Looking at risk ratings. During the quarter, we downgraded the Cambridge Life Science and San Diego multifamily loans to risk rating 5. As a result of these developments, we recorded total incremental CECL provisions of $44 million during the quarter.

These two properties were on Risk Rating 4 before; now they are on Risk Rating 5 and provisions:

We can see the migration of the Cambridge building and the San Diego property to the Risk Rating 5 category. The Chicago office building is still in Risk Rating 4, but we are fairly conservative to assume this will eventually get impaired as well.

As per the fund disclosures, there are currently no other assets in the Risk Rating 4 category:

Given the seasoning, we are hopeful this will be the end of the rating migration, but as we stated above, one should expect some sort of impairment eventually from the Chicago Office Risk 4 asset (think somewhere around a $10-$20 M hit). Looking through the tape of loans the fund has, there are still a number of 2021 and 2022 issued loans, so unfortunately it is not out of the question to see more loans go into category 4 as maturity dates approach.

Given the new impairments, the book value was hit, and kept moving lower.

Now the common shares trade at $7.4/share as of the writing of this article, telling us the market still sees plenty of downside for the remaining portfolio here.

One of the most important segmentations that the fund does not show in its presentation is the loan vintage. We have done this ourselves by extracting the loan tape in Excel:

The 'problem years' for origination are 2020 and 2021 when rates were very low and values for properties high. We have highlighted 2022 in blue because some of the loans for the year 2022 were done later in the year, thus already embedding higher rates and lower property values. Irrespective, we can see that almost 60% of the remaining holdings are still from 'problem years.'

This simply means there could still be a rating migration to Risk Rating 4 or 5 and impairments down the line. Until we see the 'problem years' percentage go below 20% of the holdings, we think the market will still discount the common shares very heavily. In essence, the market is purely doing a 'mark to market' on potential future impairments.

It is encouraging to see the fund allocate capital to 2025 vintage loans since it is ideal to underwrite CRE credit in today's depressed value conditions. We would like to see more 2025 and 2026 loans in the holdings.

Earnings are almost covering distributions

While impairments are non cash income statement items (unless a loan gets sold), we can see the new originations helping the fund almost cover the common shares distributions:

Ignoring impairments, the fund made $0.22 per share while distributing $0.25. Unless there is a cascade of new impairments, do expect the fund to keep distributions at the current levels since they are almost covered.

REO not doing well

Remember that when a fund like KREF takes over a building because the holder defaulted on the loan, the building becomes real estate owned ('REO'). The fund has a number of such properties, but they are generating a negative net income:

For the fiscal year 2025, the company had a -$9 M hit from REO buildings (revenues from REO minus expenses from REO). This purely means the assets are generating negative income, in addition to potentially having an impact on the income statement upon sale (positive or negative depending on fair market values versus where the fund carries them).

KKR Real Estate understands this issue, and in a recent interview stated that it intends to reduce REO assets and watchlist assets:

As per their own statement, "The overall goal is to compress the discount of our stock price to book value". KREF fully understands the market is pricing the uncertainties associated with true fair value for these properties via the large discount in the common shares.

Cleaning up uncertainties on the balance sheet will most certainly close the gap on the discount for the common shares, while opening up the balance sheet for more healthy 2026 vintage lending. Do note that if the fund ends up selling at very sizable discounts to what the book value says, then the opposite will occur. In order for the market to believe what the fund is saying, they need to unload problem loans at carrying value minus taken impairments (i.e., true book). If it turns out that the market based fair value is much lower, then the current discount to book is fully justified.

Preferred shares - a cheap form of permanent capital

The fund's debt maturity profile looks as follows:

There is a big wall in 2027 on the mortgages themselves, with corporate debt coming due only in 2032. Against this backdrop, consider the terms of the preferred shares:

  • Term: perpetual
  • Type: cumulative
  • Coupon: 6.5%
  • Current Yield: 9.2%

The preferred shares are perpetual, and they were done at a low coupon of 6.5%. We are of the opinion they will represent a permanent form of capital going forward, given they were issued when rates were very low (in 2021).

Now when it comes to KREF.PR.A, it is all about risk/reward. Should you buy them at a current yield of 6.5%? Absolutely not. Are they appealing at a yield of above 9%? We think yes.

The preferred shares have roughly $0.46 billion in market value common equity under them, and $0.84 billion in 'book value' common equity under them. An investor should only worry here if they think KREF is going insolvent, which we think is not the case.

We are of the opinion that KREF will muddle through, and the KKR name should not be discounted. We saw how Apollo Commercial Real Estate Finance (ARI) sold its portfolio close to book value to an Apollo affiliate, thus speaking volumes for the power of these alternative asset managers. Push comes to shove, KKR will inject equity or buy some of the assets close to book value to keep KREF afloat. The hit to the brand is much higher than keeping KREF going.

All in all, we like KREF.PR.A at a yield above 9%, and if we look historically, the securities have bottomed at close to 10% yields in the midst of heavy bear markets:

The securities have been stuck in a range over the past few years, with $16/share being the bottom and $22/share the top. Buying when close to the bottom of the range and selling when close to the top of the range is a good strategy here.

Now, for comparison purposes, also think that CCC HY bonds also yield 9%:

We would rather hold KREF.PR.A, with common equity under us and the KKR backing rather than CCC HY bonds.

Conclusion

KREF had another tough quarter in Q4 2025. The fund took more impairments, depressing its book value further. The crux of the issue lies with the 60% holdings, which are 2020/2021/2022 vintage loans, securities that can still result in more losses. The market is doing the impairments for the fund, with the common equity trading at roughly 57% of book value. On the positive side, the fund is lending more, with a high proportion of 2025 vintage loans, which are book accretive for us.

While common share fair value is a complex distressed opportunity, what is fairly appealing in the capital structure is the preferred equity. KREF.PR.A is now trading at a 9.2% current yield, wider than CCC HY credit. We find that level to be very appealing given the fund sponsor and track record, and are a buyer of the preferred securities here (which we own).

data.title: Amgen: Wait For A Better Entry Point (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Back in November 2025, I initiated coverage on Amgen Inc. (AMGN) with a buy rating. I viewed them as a cheap biotech leader as I saw strong sales growth, an impressive pipeline, and a discounted valuation relative to sector peers. The stock has gained a very respectable ~18% since then, and that is clearly shown in the rating history chart below. Amgen reported earnings back in February, and so today I'll be analyzing their fundamentals to see if the bull thesis remains intact now.

Below, it is shown that the long-term looks quite bright for Amgen, as their pipeline of innovative products is impressive. However, the company's financial performance is quite mixed, as growth is slowing and AmgenNow discounts look like they are taking a toll on the company's gross margin. Moving forward, guidance for 2026 is less than ideal. With the valuation having climbed since my previous article, the risk/reward now seems to be around neutral, with current levels not offering a good entry point. Therefore, I have decided to downgrade to a hold rating.

Pipeline Looks Strong

Before looking at their financial results for 2025 Q4, it is important to look at their pipeline since it is usually a key indicator of the company's future and long-run performance.

Above, you can see Amgen's Phase 3 pipeline for the four key therapeutic areas. They have strong candidates in each of the categories, and for rare diseases, Tepezza and Dazodalibep are both expected to have study completions in H2 2026. Therefore, there is potential for approval in the not-so-distant future, and if the treatment is indeed successful, the financial results will follow.

One of the most exciting pipeline products for Amgen is likely MariTide. Currently, there is no shortage of weight management options, but Amgen believes they bring a differentiated product to the table. In their presentation at the TD Cowen 46th Annual Health Care Conference, Amgen's Senior Vice President of Global Marketing & Access Kave Niksefat stated that

In a field featuring dozens of potential daily oral and weekly injectable medicines, MariTide stands alone as the only therapy in late-stage development to offer the paradigm-changing prospect of strong efficacy and favorable tolerability at monthly, every other month or even quarterly dosing.

Therefore, it seems that down the road Amgen will be in a solid position to compete against the current market leaders such as Novo Nordisk's (NVO) Ozempic. While the competition in the weight management space is fierce, the opportunity is also sizeable for a company like Amgen whose product offers significant advantages and differentiation.

Net Sales Of Key Products

In my previous article, I highlighted net sales for Repatha and for Tezspire. For Repatha, the company was reported to be offering it at a discount to U.S. patients through AmgenNow. Despite this, net sales growth still accelerated from 40% back in Q3 to 44% in Q4. This indicates that the discount hasn't slowed their business momentum for this key product. As you can see above, Repatha remains one of their top offerings by revenue.

As for Tezspire, the company got FDA approval for the treatment of chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps in patients 12 and older last October. For this treatment, net sales growth accelerated considerably from 40% to 60%, and so it seems that adoption has been robust after the expansion of use cases. This is a testament to the effectiveness of Tezspire in new areas.

Speaking of new approvals, on Feb 12th, a press release showed that the European Commission had approved Uplizna for generalized myasthenia gravis. Amgen stated that this is the "First and Only CD19-Targeted Therapy Approved in Europe for adults with anti-AChR+ and anti-MuSK+ gMG." Clearly, this treatment is innovative and will make a difference for patients. Financially, you can see above that this product isn't one of their best sellers yet, but moving forward, this approval should help to drive growth over time.

Financial Performance Is Mixed

Let's now take a close look at the financial performance of Amgen in Q4. Firstly, revenues were reported to be $9.866 billion, up 9% YoY. This was a modest deceleration of growth from the 12% seen in Q3 and so despite the strong performance of Repatha and Tezspire, overall business momentum slowed a bit. Still, Amgen was able to beat top line expectations by $406.11 million, and so results were much better than feared.

In addition to the deceleration of growth, another potential concern may be margin erosion. As shown above, non-GAAP operating expenses increased by 16%. Breaking down these expenses, there are some good signs and some not-so-good signs. Starting with the good signs, in Q4, Amgen boosted R&D spending YoY as this category saw an increase from 19.5% to 22.8% as a percentage of product sales. This shows that the company continues to invest heavily in biotech innovation, and that should build a strong pipeline moving forward. Long term investors should be encouraged by that. The other good sign was that SG&A expenses decreased as a percentage of product sales. This indicates that the company is being run in an efficient manner.

As for the bad news, cost of sales is up quite significantly versus revenues. This could be the result of the discounts offered through AmgenNow. They state in their earnings release that while they saw 10% volume growth, that strength was partially offset by 4% lower net selling prices. While Amgen doesn't like to refer to their gross margin, there has clearly been deterioration in this key metric. At the end of the day, profitability was probably softer than desirable, with operating margin dropping by 350 basis points and adjusted EPS showing no growth YoY. Still, they beat bottom-line EPS expectations by $0.56.

Annual Outlook Is Not Ideal

For 2026, they expect revenue of around $37.7 billion at the midpoint. That implies annual growth of 3%. Furthermore, that's a deceleration from 2025's 10% growth rate, and so there are some signs of sluggishness in their business.

As for non-GAAP EPS, the midpoint of $22.30 would represent a 2% increase. Firstly, this is also quite the slowdown from the 2025 bottom line growth rate of 10%. Secondly, this would underperform the projected top-line increase for 2026 modestly. This indicates that profitability will remain shaky, probably because of the drug discounts the company is having to offer due to political pressures from the Trump Administration.

As a whole, while the top and bottom line guidance midpoints were actually both above consensus expectations, I find the outlook for 2026 to be a bit disappointing, with both growth and margin not on great trajectories right now. Political pressures seem to be catching up to the company.

Valuation Has Expanded

In my previous article on Amgen, the forward P/E ratio was at 14.95. As you can see above, the multiple has now expanded to 16.95 and is quite elevated relative to the past few years. I believe there are reasons to turn a bit cautious right now.

Today, I found a weakening in Amgen's financials. While the pipeline is encouraging for the long term, the company seems to be dealing with challenges. Growth and profitability both looked shaky for Q4 and guidance for 2026 wasn't ideal in my view. It seems that the political headwinds mentioned back in November are material, and for the near term I believe the company's prospects have dimmed somewhat.

Meanwhile, the stock's valuation has climbed notably to a historically elevated level. In fact, the forward P/E just set a multiyear high recently. Therefore, I believe the risk/reward has deteriorated somewhat since my previous article, and the stock does not offer a great entry point for investors anymore.

Concluding Remarks

As stated at the beginning, I called Amgen a biotech leader in my previous article. After today's analysis, I still believe the company is one of the best in this space, but currently I would encourage investors to hold off on buying. The stock has seen a good deal of outperformance lately, and with the near-term prospects dimming and the valuation having expanded, opportunity has shrunk significantly in my view. The company remains robust enough for long-term investors to keep holding, but prospective investors probably want a better entry point. As a result, I have decided to downgrade to a hold for now.

data.title: SES S.A.: Integration, Defence-Driven Growth, C-Band Upside Reinforce Our Investment Case
data.description:
As our readers know, we only publish a limited number of initiation notes each year. SES S.A. (SGBAF) was among them, and the stock has delivered solid performance since the end of 2025. We are up 22.58% on solid numbers released on March 2 (Fig. 1).

2025 was a transformative year for SES, given Intelsat's acquisition (closed on July 17, 2025). Our equity story was underpinned by SES's successful transformation into a global multi-orbit leader. Aside from a management focus on integration, higher synergies, and a deleveraging story, our main investment thesis was supported by potential upside from the U.S. C-band spectrum monetisation. In our last analysis, this was calculated to be €3.53 billion after tax (higher than SES's market capitalisation).

Fig. 1

SES Results and Our Upside

The SES story is increasingly a tale of two businesses. The Networks segment is the clear growth engine, delivering its fourth consecutive year of growth (on a like-for-like basis) and now representing 62% of the total top-line sales. SES results were driven by solid momentum in Government and Aviation, which increased by +47% and +145.5% year-on-year, respectively, on a reported basis. As shown in Fig. 2, the Fixed & Maritime division is navigating competitive headwinds. Still, overall, the Networks segment secured an impressive €1.4 billion in new business and renewals in 2025, building a robust backlog of €3.6 billion.

The Media business, while facing structural headwinds (Fig. 2), delivered in line with expectations and remains highly cash-generative. Reported top-line sales grew 7.9% year-on-year due to the Intelsat consolidation, but on a like-for-like basis, the company's turnover declined by 12.6%. This decline was expected and is driven by capacity optimization in mature markets, the switch-off of SD channels, and the impact of a Brazilian customer bankruptcy. Despite these challenges, the business demonstrated its resilience by securing approximately €450 million in key long-term renewals in 2025 and maintaining a strong gross backlog of €3 billion.

All in all, the company delivered top-line sales of €2.62 billion and adjusted EBITDA of €1.19 billion.

Fig. 2

Why are we positive?

Reported adjusted EBITDA grew 19.1% for the full year (Fig. 2), but the margin declined to 45.4% from 51.4% in 2024. The margin profile reflects a company in transition. This dilution was anticipated and is a direct result of integrating Intelsat's business, which has a different service mix, and is also impacted by lower-margin equipment sales tied to new Aviation installations. That said, this margin pressure is offset by an aggressive synergy plan that management is fast-tracking. The company is on track to deliver €370 million in annual run-rate synergies, split between €210 million in OPEX savings and €160 million in CAPEX savings. The progress is a key pillar of our investment thesis. Looking ahead, 2026 CAPEX is guided to be approximately €700 million (Fig. 3), which is €100 million lower than prior guidance. This notable CAPEX beat demonstrated early progress on synergies.

Fig. 3

SES provided a stable outlook for 2026, guiding for both revenue and adjusted EBITDA to be stable. Aside from the disciplined approach to CAPEX, the company remains committed to achieving an IG balance sheet, targeting a net leverage below 3x. As of year-end, the combined like-for-like net leverage stood at 3.9x (this includes lease liabilities - Fig. 4). Considering an FCF of 500 million per year, the company has stated that once its leverage target is met, the majority of future exceptional cash flows will be prioritized for shareholder returns, including an increase to the base dividend. In our calculation, SES net leverage is expected to fall below 3x in 12-18 months, that is by 2027 (2.8x including lease liability). With a DPS base of €0.50, this yields 7.6%. On a capital allocation priority, here at the Lab, we like the SES approach: 1) deleverage (SES 2025 net debt is already lower than our initial forecast) and 2) higher remuneration. Even applying an unchanged valuation multiple, the company's equity value will increase.

Fig. 4

Lastly, two recurring investment themes that were central to our initial decision to initiate coverage have not yet been addressed. 1) We report the CEO update to the FY 2025 call. SES reported that on the US C-band, SES is "progressing very, very well." The CEO added that they "expect now FCC to move in the second half of 2026 to issue their ruling." This is what we are waiting for, and it is the reason for our investment. 2) EU countries and NATO allies confirmed that they "will allocate at least 3.5% of GDP annually...on defence expenditure by 2035." Defence is a central topic today, but what stands out in SES's case is its role in data intelligence, a form of passive defence that we view particularly favourably. Given Intelsat's acquisition, the company is now more exposed to multiple defence themes, such as Missile Warning & Tracking and Sovereign Networking (Fig. 5). These capabilities are essential for government and military operations. Therefore, we view SES as a strategic investment for both the US and the EU.

Fig. 5

Valuation

In our last assessment (23/Dec 2025), we reported an attractive SES valuation, implying "not assuming significant near-term" and an EBITDA of €1.22 billion in 2026. On a reported basis, SES 2025 EBITDA reached €1.5 billion; however, on an adjusted basis, the company reported EBITDA of €1.19 billion, in line with our previous estimates. Therefore, we do not change our financial forecasted numbers (at this stage). More importantly, compared to our previous estimates, we implied total debt (including lease) of €7.5 billion, while the company's net debt is at €6 billion (including cash and lease). Therefore, continuing to apply a 7.5x EV/EBITDA in our 2026 assumption, we derive an enterprise value of €9 billion. In our calculation, we also include the upside from the U.S. C-band spectrum monetisation, estimated at €3.53 billion. Therefore, with a total enterprise value of €12.53 billion and a debt of €6 billion (not including SES's potential 2026 deleveraging), our equity value reached €6.53 billion. Compared to a current market cap of €2.9 billion, we increased our share price to €14.5, remaining buyers.

Risks

Our main risks include 1) a lower US C-band auction and potential delay, and 2) lower top-line sales from Governments. We reported declining sales in the Media business and another risk: lower-than-expected cost synergies. SES is subject to several regulatory and technological risks. There is a capex risk, and past CAPEX investments have led to double-digit EPS/FCF losses. This is due to the significant CAPEX burden to operate. Additionally, we reported on new technological developments that might disrupt the satellite business.

Conclusion

Last time, we reported that the SES investment case was backed by Defence Demand (a key topic nowadays). The Q4 and full-year 2025 results show this strategy is progressing, with growth in Government and Aviation. This provides downside protection in the media segment. Aside from management's ability to deliver on synergies, the stable 2026 outlook supports our equity investment (with significant upside also due to U.S. C-band spectrum monetisation).

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cracker Barrel Old Country Store Surprised In A Good Way, But Still Deserves A Downgrade
data.description:
One of the most important lessons that I have learned in my roughly 18 years of being exposed to investing is that the market often goes off of expectations as opposed to absolute results. A company can see fundamental performance deteriorating, even rapidly, and still see a pop in the share price after reporting results that came in above what analysts anticipated. That does not necessarily mean, however, that you should buy. Context is everything, and at the end of the day, the trajectory the company is on and its valuation need to take precedence. Take, if you will, the case of Cracker Barrel Old Country Store, Inc. (CBRL), a restaurant chain that has historically been one of my favorites.

After the market closed on March 4th, management announced financial results covering the second quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Low revenue and earnings fell precipitously year over year; they did come in above what analysts were hoping to see. Unfortunately, the overall picture for the company looks a bit precarious right now. And while there is certainly some upside potential, I do not think that investors should view the 8.7% jump in the share price as a reason to dive in. In fact, even though the stock is up 6.6% since I called it a ‘buy’ in December of last year, which is comfortably above the 0% increase achieved by the S&P 500, and which excludes the after-hours move higher, I would argue that downgrading the stock to a ‘hold’ makes the most sense here.

A rough quarter

For the second quarter of the 2026 fiscal year, Cracker Barrel Old Country Store reported revenue of $874.8 million. Although that's a lot of money, it actually happens to be 7.9% below the $949.4 million that the company reported the previous year. Normally, such a year-over-year drop would cause investors to waver or panic. But the fact of the matter is that revenue ended up being $10.4 million higher than what analysts had hoped for. The overall drop in revenue for the company was caused by multiple things. For starters, the number of locations that it has in operation dropped from 726 to 710. The company has not been afraid recently to reduce location count in light of challenging financial performance. This is just a continuation of that trend. This does not mean that the company is avoiding opening new locations. In fact, for 2026 as a whole, it said that it would open two additional stores.

On top of the declining location count, Cracker Barrel Old Country Store was also negatively affected when it came to comparable store sales. Those dropped 7.6% year over year. When it came to the retail establishments, the hit to comparable store sales was a disastrous 9.2%. But even its restaurants didn't perform all that well, experiencing a 7.1% drop year over year. All of this weakness was fueled, according to the company, by a 10.1% plunge in traffic. In fact, revenue would have been worse had it not been for the fact that the average check size increased. Considering what we have contended with in terms of inflation as of late, it's not surprising to see management have to hike prices in order to protect margins. Management specifically mentioned that they saw an average menu price increase of 4.2% during the quarter and an identical increase when it came to the first half of 2026 in its entirety.

The sad truth is that, right now, consumer confidence levels are near all-time lows. That does not bode well for the food-away-from-home market that is already more expensive than the food-at-home market. According to one forecast provided by the USDA, conducted in February of this year, prices for all food should rise by 3.1% in 2026. Food-at-home prices will go up by only 2.5%. But food-away-from-home prices should pop 3.7%. Beef and veal prices can be expected to rise by about 5.5% this year, and even though prices were down a bit in January compared to December, they were still up 15% compared to the previous year. We should also see pork prices rise by 1.9%, and we should see small increases when it comes to fresh fruits and fresh vegetables. The biggest increases will come from sugar and sweets, as well as from non-alcoholic beverages. Those are forecasted to climb 6.7% and 5.2%, respectively, during the year. In fact, the only meaningful decrease that we are likely to see would be in egg prices, which are forecast to fall by about 27.4%.

Weak consumer confidence and relatively high inflation are not a recipe for success when it comes to the restaurant industry. So it's not surprising to me that overall traffic fell as much as it did. Unfortunately, this also meant a decline in profitability. The business reported a decline in earnings per share from $0.99 to just $0.06. But even that plunge meant that the company exceeded analysts’ expectations, with earnings per share coming in $0.26 above what was anticipated. This still did mean a drop in net income from $22.2 million to $1.3 million. Even adjusted net profits for the company worsened, falling from $1.38 to $0.25. This still was above what analysts had hoped to see to the tune of $0.55. But that doesn't change the fact that overall adjusted net profits fell significantly from $30.9 million to $5.6 million. Operating cash flow dropped from $98.1 million to $51.2 million. And even if we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a drop from $79.5 million to $55.2 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business declined from $74.6 million to $38.2 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the first half of 2026 as a whole compared to the same time in 2025. Once again, revenue, profits, and cash flows have all dropped for the company. Unfortunately, we can expect continued weakness throughout 2026. Management said, for instance, that revenue will total between $3.24 billion and $3.27 billion. That compares to the $3.48 billion that the company reported a year earlier. We should also expect EBITDA to be between $85 million and $100 million. The midpoint there of $92.5 million is actually slightly above the $90 million midpoint provided by management when they announced results for their first quarter. But still, this would mean a drop in EBITDA from the $224.3 million that the company reported for 2025. In all likelihood, this would mean adjusted operating cash flow of somewhere around $165 million.

As disappointing as all of this is, management has been working hard to make some changes. For instance, management continues to focus on adding to the number of rewards members that it has. By the most recent quarter, that number had grown to more than 11 million people. A year earlier, it stood at just 7.2 million. And back in the second quarter of 2024, it amounted to just 3.4 million. These types of programs helped to fuel repeat traffic. They build brand loyalty and are worth every penny in most instances.

Management has also been working on improving its menu and its guest experience, with the explicit goal of getting customers to be happier with going there. In fact, as the image below illustrates, the company has seen some progress when it comes to overall guest-centric metrics. On top of this, the company is working to address their interpretation that price sensitivity has been hurting traffic. So they have started focusing on value pricing and certain promotions.

One such example would be its Meals for Two offering that begins at $19.95. And another example would be limited-time offers, like its breakfast burger, carrot cake, and campfire meals. The firm is also bringing back popular legacy items such as hamburger steak, omelets and scrambles, country ham dinners, and even premium offerings like salmon. Plus, it is prioritizing higher food quality and consistency, which I can say from my last couple of experiences years ago were issues. Add on top of this the fact that management is working to cut costs, including between $20 million and $25 million in general and administrative expenses, not to mention a reduction in advertising expenses, lower capital expenditures, and more, and there is definitely a lot of potential here for a turnaround. I would have hoped, coming into this earnings release, that the company would expect at least some traffic growth this year. But that's not the case. In fact, it will likely be 2027 or later before traffic actually recovers, if at all.

Keeping this in mind, in the chart above, you can see how the stock is priced at the moment. Using the forward estimates for 2026, I wouldn't say that the stock is expensive. I wouldn't say, however, that it's inexpensive either. In fact, it looks to be pretty fairly valued considering the challenges that management continues to struggle with. In that same chart, you can see how the firm is priced compared to five other similar restaurants. It is, based on the data provided, around the middle of the group. So that reinforces the view that this is more of a fair value play than it is anything else.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided right now, it is my belief that while Cracker Barrel Old Country Store might do very well over the next several years, this year will remain a transition year for the company. I would venture to say that, as economic conditions worsen, we will see more pain than what management is hoping for. That furthers my view that we should see this as more of a ‘hold’ candidate than as a ‘buy’ one.

data.title: Small-Cap Stocks Are Stirring On Policy Shifts, Earnings Edge
data.description:
By James MacGregor, CFA, Samantha S. Lau, CFA and Erik Turenchalk, CFA

Are US small caps finally shaking off a decade of underperformance?

After years of trailing their large-cap peers, small-cap stocks have tested the patience of even seasoned investors. But we believe a dramatic improvement in earnings growth driven by lasting change in the US economy is creating sustainable recovery potential for small companies of all types.

Uncertainties over US tariff policies and GDP growth were especially strong headwinds for small-cap stocks to start 2025, given the asset class’s comparatively large ties to the US economy. But small caps surged since the second half, with the Russell 2000 Index up 22.0%, compared to 11.8% for the S&P 500, from July 2025 through February 27, 2026.

Similar rallies in previous years have fizzled out quickly. This time, we believe small cap’s notable comeback isn’t the typical “head fake” of past upticks but more the result of headwinds flipping to strong and possibly lasting tailwinds.

Small Caps Benefiting from US Economic Transition

The US Supreme Court’s recent ruling against the Trump Administration’s use of certain tariffs has left trade policy again uncertain and markets somewhat volatile. But we believe investors who look past this near-term noise will see a powerful and positive US economic transition underway. After a tariff-related pause in 2025, the US economy’s renewed domestic focus has been a magnet for corporate investment.

We believe small-cap companies stand to be the biggest beneficiaries of the US economy’s strengthening, given their relatively larger exposure to it. In fact, almost 70% of small-cap companies generate more than 90% of their sales domestically, compared to less than 40% for large companies (Display).

Growing demand for new infrastructure, especially to upgrade supply chains and energy access, should also be supportive. Smaller companies, with their improved cost structures, will now be able to meet this demand on a more even footing with larger, globally focused companies, in our view.

Onshoring Levels the Field vs. Large Caps

We believe a new paradigm is emerging with the potential to undercut the stubborn dynamic that weighed on small-cap performance for the last decade. Globalization has long benefited mostly US large-cap companies, which naturally were better positioned to leverage lower operating costs and fast-growing markets overseas.

In a more domestically focused US economy, small caps aren’t as disadvantaged in terms of cost structure and market scale. In fact, the playing field is leveling as supply chains and trade patterns lean toward US products and manufacturing. US reliance on Chinese imports, for example, is about half of what it was in 2015, while more money than ever is going to factory construction stateside.

Moreover, US manufacturing has begun to show signs of recovery. The Institute for Supply Management’s Purchasing Managers Index reached 52.4 in February, the second consecutive month it has surpassed 50, which indicates expansion. The expanding economy will likely need significant investments, too, given that the American Society of Civil Engineers pegs the country’s infrastructure—from roads and bridges to water pipes and treatment plants—at near or past life expectancy.

We think the need for massive infrastructure investment—and the fact that domestic companies are becoming equipped and incentivized to step up—is another structural tailwind, especially for smaller industrial and tech firms overshadowed by the AI rush.

US Policy Also Supportive

Ongoing policy changes are also accelerating the US economic transition. The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA), for instance, should pump significant fiscal stimulus into its economy in 2026, while the Fed’s expected course of lowering interest rates will also help.

Falling interest rates are powerful catalysts that lower the cost of variable debt, which is generally higher among small-cap companies (Display). Monetary policy easing could also free up more small-company loans, as banks tend to loosen lending requirements when debt becomes cheaper. History suggests that small-cap valuations tend to rise during Fed easing periods, and 12-month trailing price-to-earnings upticks are already underway since 2024.

As for the OBBBA, the legislation introduced more flexible guidelines for how and when businesses can depreciate equipment and real estate. This provision should be a boon to domestic manufacturers especially, since qualified production property is 100% deductible in the year it’s placed into service.

Sharper Earnings Edge Emerges

Evidence that small caps are benefiting is starting to surface in fundamental metrics. Earnings growth, for example, is expected to outstrip those for large companies in 2026, based on consensus forecasts, while revisions for US small-cap value stocks recently flipped positive (Display).

Part of the upbeat outlook hinges on falling inflation, which has a larger impact on smaller companies than on large caps. As input costs such as wage pressures moderate, for instance, projected year-over-year small-cap earnings look more compelling. Moreover, small-cap earnings could be widely underestimated by the market, creating opportunities for selective growth and value investors alike.

Another reason for optimism: investor expectations for small caps remain very modest. While large-cap valuations, fueled by their concentration in high-flying technology stocks, remain elevated, small-cap price-to-earnings multiples are hovering at historical averages of about 19x. At the same time, forward earnings growth rates between the two are almost neck and neck, which make small caps compelling given their much lower valuations, in our view.

Even after their recent bounce, small-cap valuations remain deeply discounted while showing vast improvements in earnings visibility for the first time in years. That’s a powerful combination, which we think can endure and reward selective investors, given small-caps' access to an increasingly domestic-focused US economy.

Shifting winds have already stirred up the beginning of a rotation. But it’s not too late for investors to consider adding exposure to small caps, which can help diversify equity allocations in today’s rapidly changing market conditions.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Where Global Economies Sit In The AI Stack
data.description:
By Dina Ting, CFA, Head of Global Index Portfolio Management, Franklin Templeton ETFs

US companies still dominate artificial intelligence (AI) capital and model production, but having an AI advantage is not a single leaderboard. In 2024, US private AI investment reached US$109 billion, dwarfing activity in China and the United Kingdom, which ranked as distant runners-up.1 Yet as AI investment moves from model creation to physical buildout and enterprise deployment, value creation increasingly extends beyond the United States, flowing to companies tied to global hardware supply chains, industrial systems and real-economy adoption. This shift has important implications for how investors think about global equity exposure.

China: infrastructure, manufacturing AI and platform-led deployment

China's position in the AI stack is arguably less about dominance in proprietary frontier models and more about scale, infrastructure investment, and deployment across digital platforms and manufacturing. While US firms continue to lead in AI capital at the research frontier, China is advancing a different model centered on cost-efficient, open-source and application-driven AI, exemplified by DeepSeek, which highlighted the country's ability to develop competitive large language models with far lower training costs and compute intensity.

This approach has lowered barriers to adoption and accelerated diffusion across enterprises. At the same time, China's largest internet and digital services firms are entering a renewed multi-year capital expenditure cycle, collectively set to invest more than US$78 billion through 2027, with spending increasingly concentrated on AI infrastructure, data centers and cloud capacity.2 Annual AI-related capex is projected to rise steadily through the middle of the decade, signaling a structural commitment to scaling compute rather than a one-off investment wave.

China's AI strategy is tightly integrated with its manufacturing upgrade roadmap. National policies emphasize embedding AI into smart factories, robotics and supply-chain optimization to improve productivity, reduce labor intensity and enhance global competitiveness. As a result, industrials, materials and hardware-oriented technology sectors play a central role in China's AI exposure.

Beyond the United States: Global AI Vibrancy Leaders

Weighted Index Score

Rank Top 10 Government AI Investment Commitments (cumulative 2013-2024) Primary Strategic Focuses
1 United States $471 B Generative AI, semiconductors and national security infrastructure
2 China $119 B Autonomous systems, smart manufacturing, health care AI
3 United Kingdom $28 B Public services integration, AI safety research and health care
4 Canada $15 B Supercomputing infrastructure, SME AI access, research capacity
5 Israel $13 B Defense technologies, cybersecurity AI, R&D facilities
6 Germany $13 B Industrial automation, manufacturing AI and legal frameworks
7 India $11 B Health care, agriculture, education and computing infrastructure
8 France $11 B Ethical AI frameworks, transportation and cybersecurity
9 South Korea $9 B Semiconductor AI chips, robotics and smart cities
10 Singapore $7 B Urban management, finance AI and responsible AI governance

Source: Artificial Intelligence Index Report 2025, in USD.

The Top 10 government investment commitments highlight that AI leadership is geographically diverse. The United Kingdom, Germany and India each reflect distinct strategic approaches-from public services integration and industrial automation to digital public infrastructure and computing expansion-while China's cumulative commitment underscores the scale of its long-term industrial AI ambitions. We will examine these ecosystems, including the United Kingdom's regulatory-first framework, Germany's manufacturing integration, and India's digital public stack, in greater detail in an upcoming commentary. Here, we focus more directly on where the AI stack intersects with equity market exposure, particularly across Asia and energy-enabled infrastructure markets.

Taiwan sits at a critical chokepoint in the AI compute stack, producing roughly 90% of the world's most advanced logic chips. The concentration is often described as Taiwan's "silicon shield" for its strategic global importance.

While Taiwanese manufacturers have expanded production footprints in the United States and other regions, replicating the full level of sophistication found in Taiwan's semiconductor ecosystem is expected to take many years. Advanced chip manufacturing depends not only on fabrication facilities, but also on dense networks of specialized suppliers, experienced engineering talent, advanced packaging capabilities, and rapid production learning cycles. This ecosystem depth remains difficult to reproduce quickly, reinforcing Taiwan's competitive advantage as AI-driven demand for high-performance computing accelerates.

South Korea's memory-chip segment plays a central role in the AI hardware stack, with global leadership in dynamic random-access memory, including high-bandwidth memory, and a top position in NAND flash storage. As AI spending increasingly shifts toward graphics processing units and accelerator-based servers, demand tilts toward higher-value memory configurations, and this shift toward more memory-intensive AI workloads should benefit South Korea's tech sector. But South Korea also illustrates how AI advantage does not require leading the race to build the largest global models. Instead, domestic firms are focusing on localized and industry-specific AI systems, particularly for non-English languages and regionally tailored enterprise use cases. In late 2025, the country climbed seven places in global AI diffusion rankings, driven by increased workplace integration and digital skills uptake. A new report from the AI Economy Institute identifies South Korea's two largest sectors, technology and industrials, as embedding AI tools at a faster pace than many global peers.3

South Korea's AI opportunity is increasingly visible in heavy industry, not just software. In late 2025, a major Korean industrial group touted that AI and big-data systems are being integrated across shipyard design, planning, and production workflows, with the goal of creating fully connected "smart yards" capable of predicting and optimizing output. The country's major shipbuilder already deploys digital twin replicas of physical shipbuilding facilities and is expanding these systems into end-to-end, AI-enabled production platforms. By 2030, they aim to operate an intelligent autonomous shipyard expected to boost productivity by roughly 30% and reduce construction timelines by a similar magnitude. These initiatives are designed not only to address expected labor constraints in shipbuilding but also to strengthen Seoul's competitive position relative to Beijing. For investors, South Korea's position in the AI stack is defined by deployment over dominance.

Japan: industrial AI and productivity capture

Japan's position in the AI stack is defined less by capital intensity and more by industrial deployment, execution and policy alignment. That backdrop has become more constructive following Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's sweeping electoral victory, which markets have interpreted as a strong mandate to push through structural reforms and long-term growth initiatives. Gauging by strong year-to-date net inflows into Japan-focused exchange-traded funds, early investor response has reflected optimism that clearer leadership and policy continuity could accelerate implementation in areas such as AI, advanced manufacturing and semiconductors.

While Japan does not rank among the largest government spenders on AI in absolute terms, it consistently places among the world's top AI economies in Stanford's AI Vibrancy rankings, reflecting strength across research, infrastructure and private-sector adoption. Under Takaichi's leadership, the government approved Japan's first national AI plan, committing roughly US$6.3 billion over five years to strengthen foundational AI capabilities, robotics integration and industrial deployment. The emphasis is not on chasing frontier models but on embedding AI into sectors that already anchor Japan's economy.

Importantly, Japan is also pursuing a strategy to recapture semiconductor leadership across critical parts of the value chain. Government-backed efforts, such as Rapidus, are targeting 2-nanometer production later this decade, while Japan retains world-class strength in semiconductor materials, wafers, test equipment and precision manufacturing inputs that are essential to advanced chip fabrication globally. This ecosystem depth reinforces Japan's strategic relevance within AI hardware supply chains. This positions the nation not only as a manufacturing location but as a foundational supplier of the tools and materials every advanced fab depends on.

This matters from an equity-market perspective. Japan's market is heavily geared toward industrials (roughly 28%), financials (about 16%) and consumer discretionary firms (around 14%), with technology close behind (near 14%).4 This tilt reflects an economy built on manufacturing depth and capital discipline. Few countries are as comfortable integrating robotics into daily life and industrial production, and that long familiarity with automation shapes how AI is deployed.

Brazil and Saudi Arabia: Energy abundance as an AI infrastructure advantage

Brazil's AI exposure extends beyond commodities. As we recently noted in our Brazil commentary, the country's energy mix is among the cleanest globally, with renewables accounting for roughly 90% of electricity generation. This clean energy advantage is increasingly relevant for enterprise-scale AI infrastructure, where power cost and stability directly affect data center economics.

Brazil has also emerged as a leading Latin American AI hub. With more than 7,000 kilometers of coastline, the country has direct access to major submarine cable systems linking the Americas, Europe and Africa-an increasingly important structural advantage as data intensity and cross-border digital traffic expand.

For investors, Brazil's role in the AI stack is less about frontier research and more about infrastructure conditions: clean power, connectivity, and a growing domestic digital ecosystem that supports regional AI deployment.

Saudi Arabia is pursuing one of the most ambitious state-led AI infrastructure strategies outside the United States and China. Under Vision 2030, Riyadh is leveraging abundant low-cost energy, capital and land to position itself as a regional hub for AI infrastructure and computing capacity.

In 2025 alone, the government announced nearly US$15 billion in AI and digital transformation investments, with significant capital directed toward data centers, cloud buildout and AI commercialization.

For investors, Saudi Arabia's position in the AI stack lies in its ambition to become a supplier of enabling infrastructure-power, data center capacity and digital connectivity-rather than solely an end user of AI systems.

More broadly, AI should be viewed as a geographically dispersed, layered buildout across semiconductors, power, automation and digital infrastructure. As spending shifts from model development to deployment, the set of beneficiaries broadens-and so does the corresponding investment map.

Endnotes

  1. Source: Artificial Intelligence Index Report 2025.
  2. Source: Bloomberg Intelligence, February 9, 2026.
  3. Source: "Global AI Adoption in 2025-A Widening Digital Divide." Microsoft: AI Economy Institute. January 8, 2026.
  4. Source: Bloomberg, as of February 2026. The MSCI Japan Index is designed to measure the performance of the large- and mid-cap segments of the Japanese market. With 183 constituents, the index covers approximately 85% of the free float-adjusted market capitalization in Japan. The FTSE Japan RIC Capped Index represents the performance of Japanese large- and mid-capitalization stocks. Securities are weighted based on their free float-adjusted market capitalization and reviewed semi-annually. Indexes are unmanaged and one cannot invest directly in an index. Important data provider notices and terms available at www.franklintempletondatasources.com.

What are the risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal.

Equity securities are subject to price fluctuation and possible loss of principal.

ETFs trade like stocks, fluctuate in market value and may trade at prices above or below their net asset value. Brokerage commissions and ETF expenses will reduce returns. ETF may not readily trade in all market conditions and may trade at significant discounts in periods of market stress.

International investments are subject to special risks, including currency fluctuations and social, economic and political uncertainties, which could increase volatility. These risks are magnified in emerging markets. Investments in companies in a specific country or region may experience greater volatility than those that are more broadly diversified geographically. The government's participation in the economy is still high and, therefore, investments in China will be subject to larger regulatory risk levels compared to many other countries.

Investment strategies that incorporate the identification of thematic investment opportunities, may be negatively impacted if the investment manager does not correctly identify such opportunities or if the theme develops in an unexpected manner. Investments focused in the technology and information technology industries carry much greater risks of adverse developments and price movements than investments in a wider variety of industries.

WF: 9076154
